html:
GAF's Commercial Line: EverGuard and What Sits Behind the Name

GAF is a name most building owners already recognize from residential shingles, but their commercial roofing division is a separate product catalog entirely, covering single-ply membranes and modified bitumen systems relevant to the flat and low-slope roofs common on the buildings we work on around Springfield.

GAF's Position in the Commercial Market

GAF is one of the larger roofing manufacturers in North America by volume, and their commercial division, marketed under the EverGuard name, produces TPO and PVC membrane products alongside their long-standing modified bitumen line. Because GAF has scale advantages in raw material sourcing and distribution, their commercial products show up frequently in competitive bid comparisons, and it's worth knowing what's actually in the EverGuard catalog rather than treating GAF as a single undifferentiated product. That scale can be a genuine advantage on larger distribution and industrial jobs in this market, where material lead time and volume pricing matter more on a 100,000-square-foot roof than they would on a small retail strip reroof.

EverGuard TPO

GAF's TPO product line covers standard commercial mil thicknesses in both mechanically-attached and fully-adhered configurations. As with any TPO regardless of manufacturer, the mil thickness specified within the EverGuard line, rather than the brand name alone, is what determines puncture and hail impact resistance, which matters directly in a market with the hail exposure southwest Missouri sees most storm seasons.

EverGuard Extreme

GAF also markets a heavier-duty TPO product line under the EverGuard Extreme name, positioned for roofs needing higher puncture resistance or extended durability specifications. When a spec or bid references this specific product line rather than standard EverGuard TPO, it's worth confirming the actual mil thickness and reinforcement being proposed, since the Extreme branding alone doesn't specify the gauge.

  • EverGuard TPO membrane in standard commercial mil thicknesses
  • EverGuard Extreme heavier-duty TPO product line for high-traffic or high-exposure roofs
  • EverGuard PVC membrane for chemical and grease-resistant roof applications
  • Ruberoid modified bitumen product line, GAF's longstanding multi-ply system
  • StrataGrip and related base sheet products used within their modified bitumen assemblies

EverGuard PVC

GAF's PVC line addresses the same chemical and grease-resistance use case as any manufacturer's PVC product, and it comes into that conversation any time a mechanical engineer or health-department reviewer flags rooftop kitchen exhaust, industrial exposure, and chemical contact environments where TPO or EPDM would degrade prematurely. Like all heat-welded membrane, actual seam performance on GAF PVC depends on installer weld quality and probing discipline, not the manufacturer name alone.

Ruberoid Modified Bitumen

GAF's Ruberoid line is a long-established modified bitumen product family covering SBS-modified base and cap sheets in various application methods, including torch-applied and self-adhered options. This product line predates GAF's entry into single-ply membranes by decades and remains a common choice on roofs where multi-ply redundancy is the design priority over single-sheet membrane systems. On a hail-exposed roof where redundancy matters more than the lighter installed weight of a single-ply system, Ruberoid stays in the conversation specifically because of that layered construction, base sheet and cap sheet each contributing separate waterproofing, rather than relying on one continuous membrane to do the entire job.

Reading a GAF-Specified Bid Correctly

A proposal that simply lists GAF TPO without naming EverGuard versus EverGuard Extreme, or without stating mil thickness, isn't giving you enough information to compare against a competing bid on equal footing. We name the specific product line and thickness whenever a manufacturer is specified, so the comparison between bids is actually apples to apples rather than brand name versus brand name.

Warranty and System Compatibility

Like most major manufacturers, GAF's warranty programs are generally tied to using matched components across the assembly, meaning membrane, insulation, and fasteners from their approved product list, installed per their specifications. Substituting a non-GAF insulation board to save cost on a project intended to carry a GAF warranty can jeopardize that warranty even if the membrane itself is genuine, which is a detail worth confirming before signing any manufacturer-warrantied project. We spell out every component in the assembly, membrane, insulation, cover board, fasteners, by name and manufacturer in our proposals, so an owner can verify the full system matches what the warranty actually requires before work starts, not after.

Questions on GAF Commercial Roofing Products

What's the difference between EverGuard and EverGuard Extreme?

Extreme is positioned as a heavier-duty product line within the same TPO family, but the specific mil thickness and reinforcement should be confirmed directly rather than assumed from the product name.

Is GAF's modified bitumen line still commonly used on new roofs?

Yes, Ruberoid modified bitumen remains a specified option, particularly where multi-ply redundancy is prioritized over single-ply membrane systems.

Does GAF PVC perform differently than other manufacturers' PVC?

Core PVC performance characteristics are similar across manufacturers at comparable thickness; installation quality, particularly seam welding and thorough probing after every run, is generally the larger factor in long-term performance.

Can GAF TPO be mixed with another manufacturer's insulation?

It can be installed that way, but doing so may affect eligibility for a GAF system warranty, which typically requires matched, approved components across the full roof assembly.

Is EverGuard TPO rated for high hail exposure?

Impact resistance depends on the specific mil thickness within the EverGuard line selected for the project, which we size to the building's actual hail exposure and roof size rather than defaulting to whichever base-tier product keeps a bid the cheapest on paper.
